common_name: Little Finger Eggplant (Slender Baby Asian Finger Eggplant)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Little Finger is a productive, compact eggplant that performs extremely well in zone 9 hot summers. In zone 9, transplant March–April for June–November production. Harvest fruits when 3–5 inches long and deep purple-black — do not allow to over-ripen, as the thin skin becomes tough and the few seeds develop rapidly. The plants produce prolifically in clusters, and frequent harvesting encourages continued production.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun in well-drained, fertile soil. Harvest slender purple-black fruits at 3–5 inches. Little Finger is a slender, finger-shaped Asian eggplant — a compact variety producing clusters of elongated, narrow fruits (2–5 inches long, 0.5–1 inch in diameter) of the finger eggplant type. The finger eggplant category is distinct from the large, globe-shaped Italian types and the longer Asian slicers: finger eggplants are the smallest category, producing bite-sized to small fruits in prolific clusters on bushy plants. Little Finger's small fruits are characterized by very thin, tender skin; virtually no seeds; and mildly sweet, creamy flesh without the bitterness sometimes found in large-fruited types. The thin skin means no peeling is required — fruits can be cooked whole (roasted, grilled, or sautéed) without preparation. The compact cluster-fruiting habit makes Little Finger one of the most productive eggplants per plant for its size, making it excellent for small gardens and containers. The fruits are harvested frequently (every 2–3 days at peak production), and the small size makes them ideal for stir-fries, kebabs, and preparations where bite-sized pieces are desired.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Little Finger eggplant variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Asian eggplant variety comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Eggplant Production in Florida

common_name: Ping Tung Long Eggplant (Taiwanese Heirloom Slender Purple Eggplant)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Ping Tung Long is a Taiwan heirloom adapted to hot, humid conditions, making it an excellent choice for Gulf South gardens. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer and fall harvest. Harvest fruits when 10–15 inches long and still deep lavender-purple before any brown coloration develops at the calyx. Plants benefit from staking when fruit load is heavy — the long fruits can cause branches to lean.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun in rich, well-drained soil. Harvest fruits when 10–15 inches and still vivid lavender-purple. Ping Tung Long (named for Pingtung County in southern Taiwan where it originates) is a Taiwanese heirloom eggplant representing the classic Asian long eggplant type at its finest — producing exceptionally long (12–16 inch), slender, slightly curved, vivid lavender-purple to violet fruits with thin, tender skin. The variety is among the most widely grown and highly regarded long Asian eggplants in Taiwan and throughout the Chinese-American gardening community in the United States, where it has been maintained as an open-pollinated heirloom for multiple generations. The fruits are characterized by extremely thin, tender skin (no peeling needed), virtually no seeds, and very sweet, mild flesh with no bitterness — a flavor profile considered superior for raw preparations and quick stir-fries where the tender skin softens immediately without extended cooking. The vivid lavender-purple color is particularly attractive in the garden and at the table. Ping Tung Long's heat tolerance and prolific production in hot conditions make it especially well-suited to southern gardens.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Ping Tung Long eggplant variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Taiwanese heirloom eggplant collection
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Eggplant Production in the Southeast

common_name: Turkish Orange Eggplant (Round Heirloom Orange-Fruited Eggplant)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Turkish Orange can be harvested at two stages — green (immature, mildest) or orange (ripe, slightly sweeter and more complex). Harvest at the orange stage for the full Turkish Orange experience; at this stage the skin may show some streaking or mottling. In zone 9, transplant March–April. The plants are ornamental as well as edible — the clusters of round, orange, tomato-like fruits are visually striking in the garden.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun. Harvest at the orange stage when fruits are 2–3 inches in diameter for full flavor development. Turkish Orange is an heirloom eggplant from Turkey that produces compact, round fruits 2–3 inches in diameter that ripen from green to a deep, vibrant orange-red — a color completely unlike any other common eggplant and more reminiscent of a small tomato or tomatillo than a traditional eggplant. This color difference signals a different flavor profile: Turkish Orange eggplants are slightly sweeter than standard purple types, with less bitterness and a slightly more complex, slightly acidic note at the ripe orange stage. The plants are productive and ornamental — clusters of round, orange fruits hanging from attractive, somewhat spiny plants create a striking garden display that frequently prompts questions from visitors unfamiliar with the variety. The flesh is firm, white to cream-colored, and suitable for all standard eggplant preparations; the round shape makes them particularly interesting for stuffing whole, hollowing and filling with rice-and-meat preparations in the Turkish culinary tradition from which the variety originates.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Turkish Orange eggplant variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Heirloom eggplant collection
Seeds of Change — Unusual eggplant variety descriptions

common_name: Casper White Eggplant (Italian Ivory White Heirloom Eggplant)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Casper white eggplant shows good heat tolerance and performs well in zone 9. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer and fall production. Harvest when fruits are 5–7 inches long and creamy white — white eggplants turn slightly yellowish as they over-ripen, which is the signal to harvest before quality declines. Do not allow to over-ripen on the plant; harvest slightly early compared to purple types.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun. Harvest when fruits are 5–7 inches and solid creamy white before yellowing. Casper is an Italian-style white eggplant — a medium-sized, oval-to-elongated variety producing distinctive ivory-white to cream-white fruits in the 4–7 inch range. White eggplant varieties have a long tradition in Italian cooking, where they are considered particularly well-suited to oven-roasted preparations and eggplant parmesan, as the white skin and flesh remain visually lighter during cooking compared to the purple-flesh types that can discolor. The flavor of Casper white eggplant is noticeably different from standard purple varieties: consistently milder, creamier, with significantly less bitterness — white-skinned eggplants in general tend toward less bitterness than dark-skinned types, though the reason may be as much cultural (bred and selected for mild flavor in the white-eggplant tradition) as biological. The flesh is very creamy and smooth-textured, making Casper an excellent choice for dips, spreads, baba ganoush-style preparations, and dishes where a mild, creamy eggplant flavor is desired without the assertiveness of purple types. The ivory-white skin also makes Casper a visually interesting garden plant and conversation piece at farmers markets.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Casper eggplant variety notes
High Mowing Organic Seeds — White eggplant variety descriptions
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Eggplant Variety Evaluation

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring before growth begins or in early fall; Solomon's Seal establishes slowly from division (2–3 years to full size) and tolerates transplanting poorly if moved in summer. In zone 9, plant divisions October–February for spring emergence. Solomon's Seal spreads slowly by rhizome to form a steadily expanding colony — space at 12–18 inches and allow 3–5 years for a full-coverage planting. Young shoots emerging in spring are edible and were used by Native Americans.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil with a pH of 5.5–7.0; mulch annually with leaf mold or compost. Solomon's Seal (Polygonatum biflorum) is a graceful native woodland wildflower and groundcover found throughout eastern and central North America, from the coastal forests of New England to the eastern Texas Piney Woods — a true shade plant that thrives in the dappled light and moist, humus-rich conditions of deciduous forest understory. The arching, elegant stems (18–36 inches) with alternating pairs of oval, parallel-veined leaves and the signature dangling pairs of small, cream-white bell flowers along the stem undersides are one of the most distinctive and beautiful sights of the spring woodland. The name 'Solomon's Seal' references the circular rhizome scars left when old stems die back, which resemble official wax seals. Solomon's Seal is a remarkable shade garden plant: it emerges reliably each spring, arches gracefully throughout the growing season, produces the bell flowers and later blue-black berries attractive to birds, and then dies back cleanly in fall. Slowly expanding clumps fill the deep shade under oaks and other hardwoods where almost nothing else will grow. Young spring shoots are edible and have a history of use by Native American peoples as a cooked green similar to asparagus.
sources: Native Plant Society of Texas — Solomon's Seal native range and cultivation
Missouri Botanical Garden — Polygonatum biflorum plant profile
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native woodland perennial guide
